title: Meta and Google Found Liable in Social Media Addiction Cases — What It Means for META's New Dividend
description: A California jury found Meta and Google liable in social media addiction cases. With META stock down 8% and the company just 2 years into paying dividends, here's what income investors should know.
lede: A California jury found Meta Platforms and Google liable this week in landmark social media addiction cases, sending META shares tumbling nearly 8% to $525. For the growing number of income investors who bought Meta for its new dividend — now yielding 0.42% with a quarterly payout of $0.525 per share — the question is whether this legal exposure threatens the cash flow that funds those payments.

faqs.q: Will Meta cut its dividend after the social media lawsuit?
faqs.a: Extremely unlikely. Meta's payout ratio is only 8.94%, meaning it uses less than 9% of earnings to pay dividends. Even significant legal costs would barely dent Meta's ability to sustain and grow its $2.22 annual dividend.

faqs.q: What is Meta's current dividend yield?
faqs.a: Meta pays $2.22 per share annually ($0.525 per quarter), yielding approximately 0.42% at the current price of ~$525. The company started paying dividends in February 2024.

faqs.q: How much has META stock dropped after the court ruling?
faqs.a: META shares fell approximately 8% in the week following the jury verdict finding the company liable in social media addiction cases, dropping from approximately $570 to $525.

faqs.q: Is now a good time to buy Meta for dividends?
faqs.a: Meta's dividend is one of the safest in tech with an 8.94% payout ratio and 2 consecutive years of growth. The stock drop means the yield has actually improved slightly. However, ongoing legal exposure adds uncertainty to the long-term outlook.

faqs.q: How does Meta's dividend compare to other tech stocks?
faqs.a: Meta's 0.42% yield is comparable to Apple (0.44%) and Microsoft (0.72%), but its payout ratio (8.94%) is much lower, suggesting significant room for dividend growth. Google parent Alphabet does not pay a regular dividend.

sections.heading: The Verdict and Its Market Impact
paragraphs: A jury in California found Meta and Google liable for designing products that are addictive to children, marking one of the first successful cases holding tech companies responsible for the mental health effects of their platforms. The ruling could open the door to billions in damages if it sets precedent for hundreds of similar cases pending across the country.

META shares dropped roughly 8% in the days following the verdict — wiping approximately $100 billion in market capitalization. For context, that's more than the entire market cap of companies like Goldman Sachs or Starbucks, erased in a week.

sections.heading: Why the Dividend Is Safe (For Now)
paragraphs: Meta started paying dividends just two years ago, in February 2024, with a $0.50 quarterly payment. It raised the payout to $0.525 in 2025 — a 5% increase. The most recent ex-dividend date was March 16, 2026.

The key metric for dividend safety: Meta's payout ratio is only 8.94%. That means the company uses less than 9 cents of every dollar it earns to fund dividends. By comparison, AT&T's payout ratio before its famous dividend cut was over 90%. Meta could see its earnings cut in half and still comfortably pay its dividend ten times over.

On top of dividends, Meta returned $30+ billion to shareholders through stock buybacks in 2025, for a total shareholder yield of about 1.95%. The company is generating cash far faster than it's returning it — giving it a massive cushion against legal costs.
Track Meta's complete dividend history, growth trajectory, and income projections with our Meta Dividend Analysis tool.

sections.heading: The Bigger Risk: Regulatory, Not Legal
paragraphs: Individual lawsuits, even large ones, are unlikely to threaten Meta's dividend. The company has $40+ billion in cash and generates over $50 billion in annual free cash flow. It can absorb multi-billion dollar settlements without blinking.

The real risk is regulatory: if governments mandate fundamental changes to how social media algorithms work — particularly around minors — it could reduce engagement, advertising revenue, and ultimately the earnings that fund everything. The EU's Digital Services Act is already pushing in this direction, and U.S. lawmakers from both parties have proposed similar measures.

For dividend investors, the question isn't whether Meta can pay $0.525 per quarter today — it clearly can. The question is whether the business model that generates those earnings will look the same in five years.

sections.heading: Meta vs Other Tech Dividends
paragraphs: Meta's 0.42% yield looks modest, but it's competitive for mega-cap tech. Apple yields 0.44% after 12 years of dividend growth. Microsoft yields 0.72% with over 20 years of increases. Google (Alphabet) only started paying dividends in 2024 with a 0.46% yield. Nvidia currently yields just 0.03%.

Where Meta stands out is growth potential. With a payout ratio under 9%, the company has enormous room to raise dividends — potentially doubling or tripling the payout within 5 years if it follows Apple's trajectory. That's the bull case for income investors: buy a tech giant early in its dividend history, lock in a low yield today, and benefit from years of increases.
